title: Monotheism
summary: The belief in a single, all-powerful God who created the universe and demands exclusive worship — the founding theological claim of Judaism, Christianity, and Islam that shaped the religious, philosophical, and political history of half the world, and whose logic of exclusive truth generated both civilization and persecution.
hook: Monotheism is one of the most consequential ideas in human history — and one of the most contested. It gave the world universal ethics, the concept of human dignity, and the idea that history has a direction. It also gave the world religious persecution, holy war, and the conviction that those who worship differently are damned. The same theological move that generates universal human brotherhood generates the logic of the crusade.

beginner.theConcept:
Monotheism is the belief that there is only one God — the sole creator and ruler of everything that exists, demanding exclusive worship and tolerating no rivals. This idea was not obvious or universal in the ancient world, where most civilizations worshipped many gods.

The emergence of strict monotheism was one of history's most consequential developments, and one of its most double-edged. It gave the world universal ethics: all humans are accountable to the same God, which grounds the concept of human dignity. It also gave the world a new logic of religious exclusion. If there is only one true God, then other peoples' gods are false — and tolerating false religion becomes culpable. The same idea that grounds universal human dignity has repeatedly grounded persecution.
beginner.problemItAddresses:
Monotheism addresses the problem of religious multiplicity by dissolving it. If there is only one God, there is only one ultimate truth, one source of moral law, one valid form of worship.

But it creates new problems in the same move. Jan Assmann calls this the 'Mosaic distinction' — the distinction between true and false religion. He argues it is both monotheism's greatest achievement and its most dangerous inheritance. Polytheistic systems could accommodate many gods; conquered peoples' gods were incorporated, not condemned. Monotheism cannot accommodate: other gods are idols, demons, or nothing. The exclusive claim to truth generates the logic of persecution, inquisition, and holy war — not as a betrayal of monotheism's principles but as an expression of them.
beginner.competingTraditions:
The three monotheistic traditions have developed very different accounts of what monotheism requires. Judaism emphasizes the covenant between God and Israel — the Jewish people are chosen for a particular relationship defined by the Torah's commandments. Christianity added the doctrine of the Trinity — one God in three persons — and the belief that God became human in Jesus Christ. Islam insists on the absolute oneness of God (tawhid), regarding the Christian Trinity as shirk — associating partners with God — the gravest theological error.

These different emphases generated centuries of theological debate, mutual accusation, and violence between and within the three traditions.
beginner.politicalStakes:
The political consequences of monotheism have been profound and contradictory. The claim that God is ultimate authority — above kings and emperors — generated both religious liberty and theocracy. No human authority can override divine conscience; but religious authorities claiming to speak for God can override all other powers.

The concept of human dignity — that all humans are made in God's image and therefore have inherent worth — is monotheism's most important contribution to political thought. The UN Declaration of Human Rights draws on this even in its secular formulation. But the same universalism that grounds human dignity grounds the missionary imperative: if all humans are accountable to one God, all humans should worship that God correctly. The same idea has fueled liberation theology and inquisitions.
beginner.contestedHistoryC:
The historical development of monotheism is more complex than a simple narrative of sudden revelation. Biblical scholarship has shown that ancient Israelite religion was not uniformly monotheistic from the start. The Hebrew Bible contains evidence of earlier polytheistic and henotheistic practices. The strict monotheism of the later prophets was a development within Israelite religion, not its founding premise.

Akhenaten's 14th-century BCE sun-focused religion in Egypt predates Israelite monotheism but was henotheistic rather than strictly monotheistic, and collapsed almost immediately after his death. The connection between Akhenaten and Israelite monotheism — popularized by Freud — is not supported by current evidence.
beginner.whereDebateStands:
The contemporary debate about monotheism focuses on its relationship to religious violence. Assmann's Moses the Egyptian (1997) and The Price of Monotheism (2010) argued that the Mosaic distinction is the theological source of religious intolerance. Historians challenge this, pointing out that polytheistic societies also engaged in religious violence. Theologians argue the problem is specific political conditions, not monotheism itself.

John Hick's religious pluralism — the argument that all major religions respond to the same transcendent reality — represents the most influential attempt to develop tolerance consistent with monotheistic commitments.

general.theConcept:
Jan Assmann's concept of the 'Mosaic distinction' is the most important recent contribution to the intellectual history of monotheism. Assmann argues that polytheistic religions were inherently tolerant: gods could be identified across cultures (Zeus = Jupiter), and religious differences did not require resolution. Monotheism introduced an exclusive truth claim that made religious difference a theological problem — one that could only be resolved through conversion, persecution, or uneasy coexistence.

Karen Armstrong's A History of God (1993) provides the most accessible scholarly account of how the concept of God developed across the three Abrahamic traditions. Her account shows the development from the tribal deity of early Israelite religion to the universal creator God of later Judaism, Christianity, and Islam — a development that was neither smooth nor inevitable.
general.problemItAddresses:
The relationship between monotheism and natural law is one of the most important connections in Western intellectual history. The Stoic concept of moral rules binding all humans, combined with the Jewish and Christian concept of a universal creator God, produced the natural law tradition. It runs through Aquinas, Grotius, Locke, and into international human rights law.

The claim that all humans are made in God's image grounds the claim that all humans have inalienable rights. This claim has survived the secularization of Western political thought, but its philosophical basis has become contested. If the image-of-God premise is removed, what grounds the equal dignity of all humans? This question remains one of the most important in secular political philosophy.
general.competingTraditions:
Mark Smith's The Origins of Biblical Monotheism (2001) provides the essential scholarly account of how Israelite monotheism developed from earlier Canaanite polytheism. Yahweh was originally one member of a divine council. Other gods were gradually absorbed into or eliminated by the monotheistic tradition. The strict monotheism of Second Isaiah (sixth century BCE) was a specific theological development in response to the Babylonian exile — not the founding premise of Israelite religion.

This developmental account — monotheism as historical achievement rather than original revelation — has substantially revised how the Hebrew Bible is read. It also reframes Assmann's Mosaic distinction: if exclusive monotheism was a late development within Israelite religion, the distinction between true and false religion was itself a historical construction, not a foundational religious category.
general.politicalStakes:
The relationship between monotheism and political authority has been one of the most consequential questions in Western history. The claim that God is sovereign above all earthly rulers generated the conflict between church and state that shaped medieval European history and produced the Reformation. The concept of martyrdom — preferring death to apostasy — is a specifically monotheistic category: it makes sense only if there is a single truth for which death is preferable to denial.

The political theology of rights — that humans have dignity before God that no earthly power can violate — is monotheism's most productive political legacy. It grounds both the resistance to tyranny and the missionary imperative to extend the true religion to those who lack it. These two implications — religious liberty and religious coercion — both follow from the same theological premise.
general.contestedHistoryC:
Rodney Stark argued in For the Glory of God (2003) that monotheism was the distinctive feature enabling European science and capitalism. The belief in a rational, lawgiving creator God generated the expectation that nature was rationally ordered, making science possible, and the moral framework enabling market transactions. This argument has been influential in sociology of religion but challenged by historians. They point to Islamic and Jewish scientific achievements under the same monotheistic premises, and to the complex relationship between religious institutions and early modern European science.

The challenge to Stark illuminates a broader problem: monotheism is internally diverse enough that almost any claim about what it enables or prevents can be countered with historical examples from within the tradition. The three Abrahamic religions share the Mosaic distinction but have developed very different political, scientific, and economic cultures.
general.whereDebateStands:
The 'new atheism' of Dawkins, Hitchens, and Harris targeted monotheism specifically — arguing that belief in a personal God who intervenes in history and demands exclusive worship is particularly dangerous, and that its truth claim is empirically false. The argument draws on Assmann's analysis of the Mosaic distinction but deploys it as critique rather than historical description.

The theological response — from Alvin Plantinga, Keith Ward, and N.T. Wright — argues that the new atheists misunderstand what sophisticated monotheistic traditions actually claim about God. The debate turns on what monotheism entails as a metaphysical claim. If God is not a being among beings but the ground of being itself, then the empirical critique misses its target. Whether this response rescues monotheism's core claims or retreats from the personal-God commitment that has historically defined it is one of the liveliest questions in contemporary philosophy of religion.

advanced.theConcept: The philosophical analysis of what monotheism requires — and whether the concept of an omnipotent, omniscient, perfectly good personal God is coherent — has been one of the central preoccupations of analytic philosophy of religion since the 1950s. The problem of evil (if God is omnipotent and perfectly good, why does suffering exist?) is the canonical challenge. Alvin Plantinga's free will defense, John Hick's soul-making theodicy, and Marilyn McCord Adams's work on horrendous evils represent the main philosophical responses within the monotheistic tradition.
advanced.problemItAddresses: The relationship between Abrahamic monotheism and philosophical rationalism — the long tradition of attempting to reconcile faith and reason — is one of the most important intellectual projects in Western history. Maimonides's Guide for the Perplexed, Aquinas's synthesis of Aristotle and Christian theology, and Islamic falsafa (the Arabic philosophical tradition drawing on Greek philosophy) all represent attempts to show that monotheistic faith is compatible with or requires rational philosophy. The tension between revealed religion and philosophical reason — between Jerusalem and Athens — has structured Western intellectual history.
advanced.competingTraditions: The comparative theology of monotheism across Judaism, Christianity, and Islam has been substantially developed by scholars including Francis Watson, Miroslav Volf, and the contributors to the 'Abrahamic religions' dialogue tradition. The question of whether the three traditions worship the 'same God' — whether Yahweh, the God of Christianity, and Allah are identical — has political and theological implications that the comparative theology literature addresses with varying degrees of philosophical rigor; Volf's rigorous theological case for sameness contrasts with less systematic treatments that assert unity without engaging the doctrinal differences over Trinity and prophethood.
advanced.politicalStakes: The secularization thesis — the prediction that modernity would progressively reduce religious belief and practice — has been substantially revised in light of evidence that religion remains central to public life in most of the world. The persistence of monotheistic religion as a political force — in Islamic politics, in American evangelicalism, in Israeli religious nationalism — requires explanation that the simple secularization narrative cannot provide. José Casanova's Public Religions in the Modern World (1994) is the essential sociological account of how religion has 'deprivatized' in contemporary politics.
advanced.contestedHistoryC: The relationship between monotheism and empire has been analyzed by scholars including Clifford Ando (a historian of Roman religion and law) and Guy Stroumsa (a scholar of early Christianity and religious transformation in late antiquity). The Roman Empire's relationship with Judaism and early Christianity — initially persecution, then (under Constantine) adoption of Christianity as the imperial religion — represents the first major case of a monotheistic religion becoming a state religion with imperial backing. The consequences — the Christianization of the empire, the persecution of paganism and heresy, the definition of orthodoxy — represent the political consequences of the monotheistic exclusive truth claim when backed by state power.
advanced.whereDebateStands: The question of whether monotheism is uniquely prone to violence — whether the 'Mosaic distinction' generates a tendency toward persecution that polytheism lacks — is actively debated. Regina Schwartz's The Curse of Cain (1997) argued that monotheism's logic of scarcity (one God, one people, one truth) generates violence against outsiders. William Cavanaugh's The Myth of Religious Violence (2009) challenged this: religious violence is not uniquely religious but reflects political conditions, and the category 'religion' is itself a modern construction that misrepresents the historical reality.

foundation.theStory: Monotheism matters as the theological foundation of three world religions that together claim the allegiance of more than half of humanity — and as the conceptual source of both universal human dignity and religious persecution. Understanding it requires holding both its creative achievements and its violent potential without reducing it to either.
foundation.theDebate: The central debates concern whether the 'Mosaic distinction' generates a unique tendency toward religious violence, the historical development of monotheism within Israelite religion, the philosophical coherence of theistic concepts, and the relationship between monotheistic faith and political authority.
foundation.whyItMatters: Monotheism matters for understanding Judaism, Christianity, and Islam, the history of political theology, the philosophical foundations of human rights, the origins of religious persecution, and contemporary debates about religion and violence.
questions: Assmann argues that monotheism's exclusive truth claim — the Mosaic distinction between true and false religion — is the theological source of religious persecution. Is this argument convincing, or does it underestimate the capacity of polytheistic societies for violence and overestimate the causal role of theology?

The concept of human dignity — that all humans are made in God's image — is monotheism's most important political legacy. Can this concept be sustained in a secular framework, or does it depend on theistic commitments that secular ethics cannot replace?

interpretation.scholarlyConversation: The essential texts are Assmann's Moses the Egyptian and The Price of Monotheism for the 'Mosaic distinction' argument, Armstrong's A History of God for the accessible developmental account, Smith's Origins of Biblical Monotheism for the biblical scholarship, and Plantinga's work for the philosophical analysis. Hick's God and the Universe of Faiths is essential for the religious pluralism response.
interpretation.evidenceAndLimits: The historical evidence for the development of Israelite monotheism is largely textual (the Hebrew Bible) supplemented by archaeological evidence that often complicates the text's narrative. The philosophical questions — whether a perfectly good omnipotent God is coherent, whether the problem of evil is answerable — are not resolvable by historical evidence. The comparative religion questions require careful attention to what specific traditions actually claim rather than generalizations about 'monotheism.'
interpretation.takingAPosition:
The strongest scholarly position on Assmann's Mosaic distinction thesis is that it correctly identifies something important about the structure of monotheistic intolerance while overstating the contrast with polytheism. Assmann is right that the claim to exclusive religious truth — the distinction between true and false religion — is distinctively monotheistic: polytheistic systems generally could not produce this claim because they lacked the theological premise that makes it coherent. He is wrong, or at least incomplete, when he implies that polytheistic societies were significantly more tolerant of religious difference in practice. The evidence from ancient Rome, Greece, and the Near East shows that polytheistic societies persecuted religious minorities, suppressed foreign cults, and enforced civic religion with considerable violence. The difference is structural — monotheism provides a theological rationale for intolerance that polytheism lacks — but this structural difference did not reliably produce different behavioral outcomes in premodern contexts.

On the relationship between monotheism and human rights, the most defensible scholarly position is that monotheistic theology was a historically necessary but not sufficient condition for the development of universal human rights claims. The idea that all humans share a common dignity because all are made in the image of one God provided the initial conceptual framework. But the actualization of universal rights required the secularization of that framework — the extraction of the rights claim from its theological grounding and its restatement in terms that could be accepted without accepting the theology. The UN Declaration achieves this extraction; it grounds rights in human dignity without specifying the source of that dignity. This is not a betrayal of monotheism's contribution but its universalization.

On the origins question — whether Israelite monotheism was influenced by Akhenaten's solar monotheism or developed independently — the current scholarly consensus is that the Egyptian connection is unproven and probably overstated in popular accounts. The monotheisms are structurally different: Akhenaten's atenism was henotheistic (elevating one god while not necessarily denying others' existence) rather than strictly monotheistic, and it disappeared within decades of his death. The strict monotheism of the later Hebrew prophets emerged from a different cultural context through a different developmental process. Freud's Moses and Monotheism is a brilliant document of psychoanalytic projection onto history, not a reliable account of religious origins.
questions: Mark Smith argues that Israelite monotheism developed gradually from earlier Canaanite polytheism — that Yahweh was originally one member of a divine council. Does this developmental account change how we should understand the theological claims of the Hebrew Bible, or is the origin of the belief irrelevant to its content?

The three Abrahamic traditions have produced both universal ethics (all humans made in God's image) and religious persecution (heretics and infidels as legitimate targets). Is this tension internal to monotheism itself, or does it reflect the specific political conditions under which monotheistic beliefs have been implemented?

research.topic: The development of monotheism within Israelite religion
research.content: The scholarly consensus — developed through biblical scholarship, archaeology, and comparative religion — holds that strict monotheism was a development within Israelite religion rather than its founding premise. The Hebrew Bible contains evidence of earlier polytheistic and henotheistic practices. The strict monotheism of Second Isaiah (6th century BCE, the Babylonian exile period) represents a specific theological development. Mark Smith's Origins of Biblical Monotheism (2001) and Frank Moore Cross's Canaanite Myth and Hebrew Epic (1973) are the essential scholarly texts establishing this developmental account.

research.topic: The relationship between Akhenaten's monotheism and Israelite monotheism
research.content: The question of whether Akhenaten's sun-monotheism in 14th-century BCE Egypt influenced the development of Israelite monotheism — raised provocatively by Freud in Moses and Monotheism (1939) and discussed by Assmann — remains unresolved. The archaeological and textual evidence for a direct connection is weak. The parallel development of monotheistic tendencies in both Egyptian and Israelite contexts may reflect broader cultural currents in the ancient Near East rather than direct influence. The debate is ongoing but most specialists are skeptical of direct Akhenaten-Israel influence.

research.topic: The subjective religious experience of the ancient Israelite prophets
research.content: What evidence would resolve this: direct first-person accounts of the prophetic experience — the specific phenomenology of what Isaiah, Jeremiah, or Ezekiel experienced when they believed they were receiving divine communication. Why unavailable: the prophetic texts in the Hebrew Bible are literary constructions that present the prophets' messages rather than their subjective experiences; the relationship between the literary texts and the historical prophets is complex and contested; and phenomenological self-report of religious experience is always shaped by cultural categories available to the reporter. What can be established: the content of the prophets' messages, the social and historical context in which they operated, and the literary forms through which their messages were transmitted. What cannot be established: the subjective experience of prophetic communication.

comparativeNarrative.name: The Revelation of Truth
comparativeNarrative.content: The Abrahamic traditions read monotheism as divine revelation — the disclosure of ultimate truth about the nature of reality, God, and humanity's relationship to God. On this reading, the development of monotheism within Israelite religion was not a human cultural achievement but a gradual divine self-disclosure that culminated in the prophets' clear articulation of one universal God. The subsequent development through Christianity (God becoming human) and Islam (God's final revelation through Muhammad) represents the continuation and completion of this revelation. The exclusivity of the truth claim is not intolerance but faithfulness to what has been revealed.

comparativeNarrative.name: The Ambiguous Achievement
comparativeNarrative.content: The humanist tradition reads monotheism as humanity's most important religious achievement — the conceptual move that grounds universal ethics, human dignity, and the idea of moral progress — and simultaneously as the theological source of the intolerance and persecution that have accompanied it. Kant's moral theology stripped monotheism of its mythological elements while preserving its ethical core. The human rights tradition draws on the monotheistic concept of human dignity while translating it into secular terms. On this reading, the creative legacy of monotheism is real but requires secular reconstruction to be preserved without the violent exclusivism.

comparativeNarrative.name: One Truth, Many Paths
comparativeNarrative.content: The comparative religion and pluralist tradition — associated with scholars including Huston Smith, Karen Armstrong, and John Hick — reads the monotheistic traditions as different cultural expressions of the same fundamental religious reality. On this reading, the exclusive truth claims of Judaism, Christianity, and Islam are theological misunderstandings of their own deepest insights: the transcendent reality to which all three traditions point exceeds any single cultural or theological formulation. Religious pluralism represents the mature monotheistic position — the recognition that the one God cannot be owned by any single tradition.

rabbitHole.label: The Hebrew Bible / Old Testament
rabbitHole.reason: The Hebrew Bible is the foundational text of Jewish monotheism and the basis of the Christian and Islamic traditions — the development of monotheism within Israelite religion is its central theological narrative.

rabbitHole.label: Islam
rabbitHole.reason: Islam's foundational theological commitment is tawhid — the absolute oneness of God — which it presents as the restoration of the pure monotheism that Judaism and Christianity had compromised through idolatry and the Trinity.

rabbitHole.label: The Islamic Golden Age
rabbitHole.reason: The Islamic Golden Age's philosophical tradition — particularly the falsafa tradition drawing on Greek philosophy — represents the most sustained attempt within Abrahamic monotheism to reconcile revealed religion with philosophical rationalism.

rabbitHole.label: The Protestant Reformation
rabbitHole.reason: The Reformation's central theological argument — that conscience answers directly to God, without priestly mediation — is a specifically monotheistic argument for religious liberty that generated both freedom of conscience and religious wars.

rabbitHole.label: Rabbinic Judaism
rabbitHole.reason: Rabbinic Judaism is the form in which Jewish monotheism survived the destruction of the Temple — developing the tradition of textual interpretation and communal practice that defined Jewish religious life for two millennia.

rabbitHole.label: The Enlightenment
rabbitHole.reason: The Enlightenment's relationship with monotheism was complex and generative — deism, natural theology, Kant's moral theology, and the secular human rights tradition all represent attempts to preserve monotheism's ethical legacy while subjecting its supernatural claims to rational critique.

reference.author: Assmann, Jan
reference.title: The Price of Monotheism
reference.annotation: The essential argument about the 'Mosaic distinction' — Assmann's claim that monotheism's exclusive truth claim is the theological source of religious intolerance is the most influential recent contribution to the intellectual history of monotheism.

reference.author: Armstrong, Karen
reference.title: A History of God
reference.annotation: The most accessible scholarly account of how the concept of God developed within and across the three Abrahamic traditions — essential for understanding monotheism as a historical and theological development.

reference.author: Smith, Mark S.
reference.title: The Origins of Biblical Monotheism
reference.annotation: The essential biblical scholarship account — Smith's analysis of how Israelite monotheism developed from earlier Canaanite polytheism substantially revised understanding of the Hebrew Bible's theological development.

reference.author: Armstrong, Karen
reference.title: A History of God: The 4,000-Year Quest of Judaism, Christianity and Islam
reference.annotation: The most accessible serious comparative account of how the concept of God has developed across the three Abrahamic traditions — Armstrong traces the theological evolution of monotheism with scholarly seriousness and cross-traditional breadth.

reference.author: Smith, Mark S.
reference.title: The Origins of Biblical Monotheism: Israel's Polytheistic Background and the Ugaritic Texts
reference.annotation: The essential scholarly account of how biblical monotheism emerged from a polytheistic background — Smith's use of Ugaritic texts to illuminate the development of Israelite religion has transformed the scholarly understanding of monotheism's origins.

commerce.title: A History of God
commerce.author: Karen Armstrong
commerce.note: Start here — Armstrong's account of how the concept of God evolved across Judaism, Christianity, and Islam is the most accessible scholarly synthesis; essential for readers coming from any of the three traditions or from none.

commerce.title: The Price of Monotheism
commerce.author: Jan Assmann
commerce.note: The essential theoretical treatment — Assmann's argument that monotheism introduced a 'Mosaic distinction' between true and false religion that generated a new form of religious violence is the most provocative recent account; short, demanding, and productive.

commerce.title: The Origins of Biblical Monotheism
commerce.author: Mark S. Smith
commerce.note: The essential scholarly account of Israelite monotheism's development — Smith shows how Israelite religion was originally polytheistic and how monotheism emerged gradually through a process of differentiation; essential for understanding monotheism historically rather than theologically.

commerce.title: Monotheism and Tolerance: Recovering a Religion of Reason
commerce.author: Jan Assmann
commerce.note: The companion to The Price of Monotheism — Assmann's argument that the Mosaic distinction can be overcome through a self-critical monotheism that acknowledges its own violence; for readers who want the constructive response to the critique.

commerce.title: God: A Human History
commerce.author: Reza Aslan
commerce.note: The most accessible popular account — Aslan traces the human tendency to anthropomorphize the divine across religious traditions; good for readers who want the broad sweep before engaging the more specialized scholarly accounts.

popularCulture.title: The problem of evil
popularCulture.description: The problem of evil — why does an omnipotent, omniscient, omnibenevolent God permit suffering? — is the most philosophically productive legacy of monotheism. It appears in the Book of Job, is formalized by Epicurus, and generated continuous debate from Augustine through Leibniz to contemporary analytic philosophy. The Holocaust has made the problem of evil not merely philosophical but existential for Jewish theology — Elie Wiesel's Night is partly a theodicy in reverse.

popularCulture.title: Crusade and jihad as monotheist violence
popularCulture.description: The Crusades and the concept of jihad are the most consequential political expressions of monotheist intolerance — the translation of the claim to exclusive truth into organized political violence. The historical record is complex: the Crusades had political and economic motivations, and jihad has multiple meanings that the polemical tradition suppresses. But the connection between monotheist exclusivism and political violence is real.

popularCulture.title: The New Atheism and monotheism's critics
popularCulture.description: Richard Dawkins's The God Delusion (2006), Christopher Hitchens's God Is Not Great (2007), and Sam Harris's The End of Faith (2004) made critique of monotheism a mass-market phenomenon — the 'New Atheism' argued that monotheist religion was not merely false but dangerous. The debate clarified the stakes: whether monotheism's historical record of violence is essential to it or contingent; whether secular modernity is itself free from analogous forms of exclusivism.

popularCulture.title: Negative theology and the unknowable God
popularCulture.description: Negative theology — apophatic theology — argues that God is so radically beyond human comprehension that only negative statements can be made about the divine: not that God is good in any sense we understand, but that God is not-evil. This tradition — prominent in Maimonides and Meister Eckhart — represents monotheism's most philosophically sophisticated response: if God is truly infinite, human language cannot capture the divine, and all positive theology is idolatry.

popularCulture.title: Monotheism and polytheism as rival systems
popularCulture.description: The long historical conflict between monotheism and polytheism — from the Israelite prophets' attacks on Canaanite religion through early Christianity's suppression of Roman paganism — raises questions about religious toleration and cultural survival. Polytheist systems generally accommodated other gods; monotheism's claim to exclusive truth made it structurally intolerant in ways that polytheism was not, though the degree of actual violence varied enormously by time, place, and specific tradition.
